name: hammurabi (lawgiver king)
shortDescription: Hammurabi, the Babylonian king, was regarded as a divinely appointed lawgiver. He is associated with justice and divine order.
description: While Hammurabi was a historical king of Babylon (reigned c. 1792-1750 BCE), in Babylonian mythology he was elevated to a figure associated with divine law and justice, acting as an intermediary between the gods and humans. This association stemmed primarily from his famous Code of Hammurabi, a comprehensive set of laws inscribed on a stele and presented as divinely inspired. The prologue to the code explicitly states that the gods, including Anu and Enlil, appointed Hammurabi to bring about the rule of righteousness in the land, to destroy the wicked and the evil-doers; so that the strong should not harm the weak. He is often depicted receiving the law from Shamash, the Babylonian god of justice, further solidifying his role as a divinely sanctioned lawgiver. This depiction served to legitimize his rule and the laws themselves, imbuing them with divine authority. Although not a god in the traditional sense, Hammurabi's association with divine justice and his role as a recipient of divine law elevate him to a deity-like status within the Babylonian pantheon, particularly in the context of legal and societal order. His legacy extended beyond his reign, shaping legal and moral concepts for centuries to come. He embodied the ideal of the just king, responsible for upholding divine law and ensuring the well-being of his people.

sources.title: Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament
sources.author: James B. Pritchard

sources.title: Law, Literature, and Society in Ancient Mesopotamia
sources.author: edited by Dennis P. Kehoe, Steven W. Cole
